title: Essay on Shinylive
text:
In Step 3 of the Module, W10 - Communicating with Dashboards (Static Dashboard + Shiny App), you learned about serverless Shiny Apps with Shinylive. What is Shinylive? Assume that you are also explaining it to your coworkers. How does it differ from the traditional Shiny app introduced in Step 2? What are the pros and cons of Shinylive in comparison with Shiny Apps? These lecture notes will become your “Essay on Shiny Live” part of the website.



Shinylive is a way to share certain Shiny applications without relying on a traditional Shiny server. Instead of requiring a full server environment to run the app in the background, Shinylive is designed to let the app run in a lighter, browser-based way that works well for teaching examples, portfolios, and small interactive demonstrations.
To explain this to coworkers, I would describe traditional Shiny as the standard approach for building interactive R apps that depend on a server process. In that model, the user interacts with the page, the server processes the request, and the updated result is returned to the browser. Shinylive changes that model by making it possible to deliver interactive examples in a more portable format, which is especially useful for static websites and educational content.






Usually depends on a server environment
Commonly deployed through platforms such as shinyapps.io or other Shiny hosting tools
Better suited for heavier computation and more advanced backend needs
Often a stronger choice for internal business tools and more complex production apps





Works well for lightweight interactive examples
Fits naturally into static websites and portfolio pages
Reduces the need for dedicated server infrastructure
Is especially useful for classroom demonstrations, tutorials, and simple public-facing apps





Shinylive offers several important benefits:

Easier to include in a static website
Good for instructional content and demonstrations
More portable for portfolio use
Simpler to share when the app is relatively lightweight




At the same time, Shinylive has trade-offs:

It may not support every feature used in traditional Shiny apps
It is less suitable for heavy server-side processing
Complex apps may still require a traditional Shiny deployment
Browser-based performance may be more limited for larger workflows




If the goal is to build a full internal tool with heavier data processing, secure access, or more advanced backend logic, traditional Shiny is usually the better option. If the goal is to create a teaching example, portfolio demonstration, or lightweight interactive app that can live inside a static website, Shinylive can be the better fit.



The main difference is that traditional Shiny emphasizes full app hosting with backend support, while Shinylive emphasizes portability and lighter deployment. Traditional Shiny gives more power and flexibility, but Shinylive makes it easier to publish interactive work as part of a web page without maintaining a full app server.



Shinylive expands the ways Shiny-style interactivity can be shared on the web. Compared with traditional Shiny, it is more lightweight and easier to integrate into a website, especially for educational and portfolio purposes. However, traditional Shiny remains the stronger choice when the application needs full server support, more complex logic, or enterprise-style deployment.

title: Home
text:
Welcome to my MSDM website. This website showcases my work in dashboards, Shiny applications, and web-based communication using Quarto.


Hello, my name is Lucynda Young. I am a graduate student in the MSDM program with an interest in analytics, communication, and turning complex data into clear audience-centered stories.



My career goal is to build a path in data analytics, digital strategy, and business intelligence, where I can combine technical analysis with strong communication. I am especially interested in roles that involve dashboards, performance reporting, and translating data into practical recommendations for organizations and communities.






R
Quarto
Shiny
Dashboard design
Data visualization
SQL
Spreadsheet analysis
Audience-focused reporting





Communication
Problem-solving
Project organization
Analytical storytelling
Presentation design






Master of Science in Data Science and Management (MSDM), add university name here
Add prior degree or additional education here if you would like




This website contains four main sections:

Home — introduces my background, interests, and goals

Dashboard — presents a static dashboard based on Ontario International Airport community engagement data

Essay on Shiny Apps — explains how Shiny can be used to build interactive analytical tools

Essay on Shinylive — explains what Shinylive is and how it differs from traditional Shiny apps




This website was created to demonstrate not only technical work, but also the ability to organize information for an audience. A strong website should help visitors move from introduction, to analysis, to explanation in a way that feels clear, purposeful, and professional.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
Alternatively, you can create a separate simple webpage to teach your coworkers how to create a Shiny App. Let’s assume that your coworkers are interested in learning about it, and assume that you are helping them. Create lecture notes that you can use to teach the person. This will become part of the website under “Essay on Shiny Apps.”



Shiny is an R package for building interactive web applications directly from R. It is especially useful when an analyst wants users to explore data instead of only reading a fixed report. A Shiny app can include filters, menus, sliders, reactive tables, and dynamic charts, which allows the audience to focus on the questions that matter most to them.
In my earlier dashboard work, I used a Shiny-style interactive approach for Ontario International Airport’s digital performance story. The main analytical question was: Which platforms, dates, and post formats drive the strongest social engagement for Ontario International Airport, and how does performance change when the user filters by network and time? That question is a strong fit for Shiny because the audience may want to compare networks, shift time windows, and evaluate different definitions of performance such as impressions, engagements, or engagement rate.



A Shiny app is helpful for coworkers because it turns a static analysis into a tool they can explore on their own. Instead of asking an analyst to generate a new chart every time they have a different question, a user can change filters and immediately see updated outputs. This makes Shiny useful for campaign reviews, stakeholder meetings, internal dashboards, classroom demonstrations, and portfolio projects.



For the interactive dashboard, the data came from two ONT Airport performance files:

Post Performance (Ontario International Airport) January 1, 2025 - December 31, 2025.csv
Profile Performance (Ontario International Airport) January 1, 2025 - December 31, 2025.xlsx

The post-level data included fields such as:

Date
Network
Post Type
Content Type
Impressions
Reach
Engagements
Engagement Rate (per Impression)
Video Views

The profile-level data included fields such as:

Date
Network
Audience
Net Audience Growth
Published Posts (Total)
Impressions
Video Views
Engagements
Engagement Rate (per Impression)

These two files work well together because one shows post-level performance while the other provides a broader account-level view over time.



A basic Shiny app has two main parts:


The user interface controls what the audience sees. This includes the title, sidebar, input controls, plots, and tables.



The server controls what the app does. It contains the logic that reacts to user input and updates the outputs.





library(shiny)

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Simple Histogram App"),
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      sliderInput("bins", "Number of bins:", min = 5, max = 50, value = 20)
    ),
    mainPanel(
      plotOutput("histPlot")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output) {
  output$histPlot <- renderPlot({
    hist(mtcars$mpg, breaks = input$bins,
         main = "Distribution of MPG",
         xlab = "Miles per Gallon")
  })
}

shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)




I would teach a coworker to create a Shiny app in five steps:



install.packages("shiny")
library(shiny)




Start with a title, one sidebar input, and one main output.



Use renderPlot(), renderTable(), or another render function in the server.



Use shinyApp(ui, server) or run the file in RStudio.



Add filters, labels, formatting, and explanations so the app is understandable for a non-technical audience.




The ONT Airport interactive app was designed to help users answer questions such as:

How did performance change over time?
Which network produced the strongest results?
Which metric should define “best” performance?
How do filters change the trend story?

Because these questions depend on user choices, an interactive format makes more sense than a single fixed chart. Shiny therefore becomes more than a display tool; it becomes an exploratory analysis tool.




Makes data interactive
Helps non-technical users explore results
Supports multiple filters and views
Improves communication in meetings and reviews
Works well for internal dashboards and teaching examples





Traditional deployment often requires a server or hosting platform
Large or complex apps can be harder to maintain
Performance may drop with large datasets or complicated logic
Good interface design still matters; interactivity alone does not guarantee clarity




Shiny is a practical way to turn analysis into an interactive experience. For coworkers, the main idea is simple: a Shiny app connects visible controls to reactive R code. Once that concept is understood, Shiny becomes a powerful tool for sharing analysis in a way that is more flexible and engaging than a static report.
